title: Mitigating Exposure Bias
concept: Scheduled sampling and reinforcement learning are common ways to reduce exposure bias in sequence generators. Boss explain ends with: 'Next up: Transformer Foundations.'

exercises.prompt: What does the term 'Exposure Bias' refer to in sequence generation?

options.text: The mismatch between perfect training data and imperfect generated data.
Running out of data to train the model.
A security vulnerability in the model's code.

exercises.explain: Exposure bias describes the training-testing mismatch where a model is trained on perfect data but tested on its own imperfect generated data.

cards.title: Bridging the Training Gap
cards.body: During training, text generators are fed perfect, human-written sentences. But when they write on their own, a single early mistake can derail the entire sentence! How do we teach models to recover when they trip over their own words?

cards.title: Mitigating Exposure Bias
cards.body: Exposure bias happens when a model is trained only on perfect human text, but is expected to generate text on its own. To fix this, we use methods like Scheduled Sampling (slowly introducing the model's own outputs during training) and Reinforcement Learning (rewarding the model for good complete sentences) to teach it how to recover from mistakes.

cards.title: The Bicycle Training Wheels
cards.body: Imagine learning to ride a bicycle. If your training wheels always keep you perfectly upright, you will panic the moment you tilt slightly in the real world. Scheduled sampling is like gradually removing those training wheels. At first, the model gets the perfect previous words (training wheels on), but slowly it is forced to practice correcting its own mistakes (training wheels off).

cards.title: Training with Mistakes
cards.body: In early training, when predicting the next word in 'The cat sat on the...', scheduled sampling gives the model the true word 'mat'. Later in training, if the model guesses 'hat' instead, we let it continue generating from 'hat' so it learns how to write a logical sentence even after making a small slip-up.

cards.title: Perfect Training is Not Enough
cards.body:
Myth: If a model is trained on millions of perfect human sentences, it will automatically know how to correct its own mistakes.
Reality: Because it never saw mistakes during training, a tiny error can confuse the model completely. We must actively train it on imperfect sequences so it knows how to recover.

cards.title: Training vs. Real World
cards.body: See how the input data source changes when we transition from standard training to mitigating exposure bias.

left.text: Input is always 100% perfect human text. The model never practices recovering from mistakes.

right.text: Input is a mix of human text and the model's own outputs, teaching it to self-correct.

exercises.prompt: What is the main cause of exposure bias in sequence generators?

options.text: The difference between using perfect human text in training versus the model's own words in testing.
The model being exposed to too many different languages at once.
A hardware issue where the computer gets too hot.
When a model is trained only on images instead of text sequences.

exercises.explain: Exposure bias happens because there is a difference (discrepancy) between training with perfect text and generating text with potential mistakes.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Reinforcement learning can help reduce exposure bias by rewarding the model for the overall quality of its final sentence.
exercises.explain: Reinforcement learning can reward the model based on the quality of the entire generated sequence, rather than just checking if individual words match the training data.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete how scheduled sampling improves training.
exercises.sentence: Scheduled sampling helps the model learn from its own mistakes by mixing in its own generated words.
exercises.explain: Scheduled sampling works by mixing in the model's own imperfect predictions, helping it learn how to handle and correct its own mistakes.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following texts shows a model that successfully avoided the repetitive errors typical of exposure bias?

options.text: "The sky is blue blue blue blue." (Teacher Forcing Only)
"The sky is blue, which is very beautiful today." (RL / Scheduled Sampling)
"The sky is blue. Dog cat run fast jump." (Random Choice)

exercises.explain: The text generated using RL rewards is natural, while the teacher forcing example is too repetitive because it got stuck. The scheduled sampling text successfully corrected a mistake.

exercises.prompt: How does scheduled sampling help a sequence generator handle its own mistakes?

options.text: By only training on extremely short sequences.
By preventing the model from ever generating text during training.
By mixing real human text and the model's own generated words during training.
By forcing the model to only look at the final word of a sentence.

exercises.explain: Awesome job! You've learned how scheduled sampling and reinforcement learning act as training wheels and guides to keep sequence models on the right track, preventing a single mistake from causing a total derailment. Next up: Transformer Foundations.
